title: Theft
subtitle: A Violation of Rights That Earns a Quranic Prescribed Punishment

verses.arabic: وَالسَّارِقُ وَالسَّارِقَةُ فَاقْطَعُوا أَيْدِيَهُمَا جَزَاءً بِمَا كَسَبَا نَكَالًا مِّنَ اللَّهِ ۗ وَاللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ
verses.translation: As for the thief, the male and the female, amputate their hands in recompense for what they committed as a deterrent [punishment] from Allah. And Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise.

verses.arabic: فَمَن تَابَ مِن بَعْدِ ظُلْمِهِ وَأَصْلَحَ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِ ۗ إِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ
verses.translation: But whoever repents after his wrongdoing and reforms himself, indeed, Allah will turn to him in forgiveness. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.
And that they will not steal and will not commit adultery.

brief_explanation: Theft is one of the very few sins for which Allah prescribed a hadd (fixed worldly punishment) directly in the Quran — the amputation of the hand — reflecting the absolute seriousness with which He guards the property rights He established. The Quran describes this punishment as a 'nakan min Allah' (deterrent from Allah), meaning it is divinely calibrated not merely to punish the thief but to protect the entire community from a culture of violation. Theft is an act of cowardice and betrayal — taking what belongs to another through deception rather than earning it — and it erodes the mutual trust that holds communities together. However, the very next verse opens the door of tawbah: one who repents sincerely and reforms is forgiven by Allah.
capital_punishment: The Quran prescribes hand amputation (cutting of the right hand) for theft when specific conditions are met: the stolen item meets the minimum threshold (nisab — equivalent to the value of a quarter dinar of gold or more according to the majority, or 10 dirhams according to Hanafis), it was taken from a secured location (hirz), and there is no ownership claim or doubt. This is agreed upon by all four madhabs. Repeat offenses escalate — second offense: left foot; subsequent offenses differ by school. The hadd is dropped if the stolen item is returned before the case reaches the judge, or if the victim pardons, according to certain schools.
